The espresso bar on Sathorn is barely wider than two people standing shoulder to shoulder, but Roots have turned this constraint into an art form. Their single-origin pour-overs rotate every few weeks — I landed on a washed Ethiopian that smelled of bergamot and lemon curd.

Order at the small counter, claim a stool by the window, and watch Sathorn hum outside. The baristas speak quietly and precisely about their beans. No laptop army here — people come to actually taste something.

Worth arriving when they open: the roast notes are sharper before the midday crowd warms the room.

Thip Samai — Pad Thai ริมถนน

A Bangkok institution that's been on Mahachai Road since 1966. The queue snakes past rows of woks blasting flame at midnight. Order the *Pad Thai haw khai* — wrapped in a thin egg net, delivered on a small plate with a wedge of lime, bean sprouts, and dried chilli.

The ritual is the point: stand in the street, unfold the omelette wrapper with chopsticks, squeeze the lime, watch the condensation bead on the plastic cup of iced jasmine tea.

They close when the noodles run out, which can be as early as 1 am on a busy night. Come after 10 pm if you want to avoid the worst of the tourist crush — the locals arrive late.

The ritual is the point: stand in the street, unfold the omelette wrapper with chopsticks, squeeze the lime, watch the condensation bead on the plastic cup of iced jasmine tea.

You enter through a narrow gate on Thai Wang Road and the reclining Buddha catches you off guard even when you already know it's there. Forty-six metres long, covered in gold leaf. The feet alone are five metres high, inlaid with mother-of-pearl images of the 108 auspicious characteristics of the Buddha.

Walk slowly from head to tail. The scale plays tricks — the nearer you are, the harder it is to perceive the whole figure. Step back against the far wall and it finally resolves into one coherent form.

Outside, the temple complex is a maze of chedis in varying states of gilded upkeep. Early morning (before 8 am) is the only window where you can hear the monks rather than the tour groups. Bring a pashmina for the shoulder-covering requirement.

Get there when they open — the roast notes are cleanest before the crowd.
Buy your ticket outside, skip the tuk-tuk touts. Bring a shoulder wrap.
Yes, Pad Thai before noon. No, it is not too early. Order the egg-net wrap.

A morning loop that moves from the quiet industriousness of Sathorn's coffee scene to the gold-and-incense world of the old city, and finishes with one of the most famous plates of noodles in Southeast Asia.

The walk between Wat Pho and Thip Samai is about 20 minutes through the old Rattanakosin district — past flower markets, school children in uniforms, and the occasional spirit-house shrine draped in jasmine garlands. Keep to the side streets rather than Chakrabongse Road and the city moves at a different speed.

Total distance on foot: roughly 4 km. Comfortable in soft shoes; the temple requires foot coverings be removed.
